title: How to parent your children differently than you were parented
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
The moment it hits you 

 You're standing in the kitchen. Your child is crying because you said no to something. Maybe it's a cookie before dinner. Maybe...
htmlContent:
The moment it hits you

You're standing in the kitchen. Your child is crying because you said no to something. Maybe it's a cookie before dinner. Maybe it's another five minutes of screen time. And suddenly you hear your mother's voice coming out of your mouth. Or your father's. That tone you swore you'd never use. That edge you remember from your own childhood. The one that made you feel small.

And you freeze. Because you promised yourself you'd be different. You promised the little version of you that you'd break the cycle. But here you are, standing in the same spot your parents stood, saying the same things they said, watching your child's face crumple the same way yours used to.

Here's the thing. That moment of recognition? That's not failure. That's the beginning.

I know because I've been there. More times than I can count. I've apologized to my kids more times than I want to admit. I've sat in my room after putting them to bed and cried because I saw my own trauma reflected in my parenting. And I've had to learn, slowly and painfully, that parenting differently than I was parented isn't about being perfect. It's about being present. It's about repairing. It's about doing the work on myself so my kids don't have to carry what I carried.

Let's talk about what that actually looks like. Because the wellness industry will sell you a lot of pretty words about breaking cycles and healing lineages. But the real work is messy. It's loud. It happens at 6:47 PM on a Tuesday when you're exhausted and your kid is screaming and every fiber of your being wants to react the way you were taught to react.

Your nervous system is the real parent in the room

Here's what I didn't understand for years. My parenting wasn't driven by my conscious choices. It was driven by my nervous system. The same nervous system that learned, before I could talk, that certain behaviors meant safety and others meant danger.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, showed us that our nervous system is constantly scanning for cues of safety or threat. This happens below our awareness. It's called neuroception. And it runs the show. When your child's whining hits that specific frequency, your nervous system might interpret it as a threat. Not consciously. But your body knows. Your heart rate goes up. Your jaw tightens. Your voice gets sharp. And suddenly you're not parenting from your prefrontal cortex anymore. You're parenting from survival.

That's not a character flaw. That's biology. Your parents' nervous systems were shaped by their parents' nervous systems. And so on. And so on. Back through generations of survival strategies that kept your ancestors alive but might not serve your relationship with your four-year-old.

So what do we do with this? We don't shame ourselves for having a nervous system. We learn to recognize when it's activated. We learn to pause. Even for three seconds. Even for one breath. That pause creates space between the trigger and the response. And in that space is where choice lives.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She walks through exactly how to recognize your nervous system's patterns and how to shift them. Not by force. By awareness. By small, consistent practices that rewire how you respond to stress.

I'll be honest with you. The first time I tried to pause before reacting to my kid, I couldn't do it. I was too fast. Too reactive. But I kept trying. I started noticing the physical sensations that came before I yelled. The tightness in my chest. The heat in my face. The urge to control. Over time, I got better at catching myself. Not perfect. Better.

And here's what surprised me. When I paused, when I regulated my own nervous system first, my child's nervous system calmed down too. Because nervous systems talk to each other. They co-regulate. Your calm can become their calm. But your panic will absolutely become their panic.

Think about it. You're not just teaching your child what to do. You're teaching their nervous system what safety feels like. That's the real inheritance.

The grief of doing it differently

Nobody talks about this part. The grief. When you start parenting differently than you were parented, you start seeing your own childhood with new eyes. You realize what you needed and didn't get. You feel the loss of what could have been. And it hurts.

I remember reading about attachment theory and realizing that my parents, who loved me the best they could, were not able to provide the kind of secure attachment I needed. They were doing what they knew. They were repeating what was done to them. But knowing that intellectually didn't stop the grief from washing over me in waves.

Here's the hard truth. You can understand why your parents did what they did and still be hurt by it. You can forgive them and still need to heal. Both things are true. Both things can live in you at the same time.

Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," spent decades showing us that trauma isn't just a story we tell. It's a physiological reality. It lives in our bodies. It shapes how we see the world and how we respond to our children. And healing it requires more than just understanding. It requires feeling. It requires allowing the grief to move through you instead of locking it away.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. She talks about the RAIN practice. Recognize. Allow. Investigate. Nurture. It's a way of being with your pain without being consumed by it. And it's helped me sit with the grief of not having the childhood I wanted for myself, while still my kids in a way that's different.

I'll tell you something that surprised me. The more I grieved my own childhood, the more patient I became with my children. Because I stopped expecting them to fill the hole in me. I stopped needing them to be perfect so I could feel like a good parent. I started seeing them as separate people with their own needs, not extensions of my wounded self.

That's the work. That's the return. Coming back to yourself so you can be fully present for them.

Repair is more important than perfection

You're going to mess up. Let's just get that out of the way. You're going to lose your temper. You're going to say things you regret. You're going to fall back into old patterns sometimes. That's not a sign that you're failing. That's a sign that you're human.

What matters is what happens after. Repair.

When you mess up, go back to your child. Apologize. Not the fake apology that blames them for making you angry. A real apology. "I'm sorry I yelled. That wasn't fair to you. I was feeling overwhelmed and I didn't handle it well. I'm going to work on that."

This is how you teach your child that mistakes are repairable. That relationships can survive conflict. That love isn't conditional on being perfect. This is how you break the cycle of shame that gets passed down through generations.

Your parents probably never apologized to you. Or if they did, it came with a side of "but you made me do it." You know what that felt like. So you know exactly what not to do.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) by Lindsay Gibson. It helped me understand why my parents couldn't apologize. Why they couldn't see me. Why they were so stuck in their own survival. And it helped me see that I could choose a different path.

Repair also means repairing with yourself. When you mess up, don't spiral into self-hatred. Don't tell yourself you're a terrible parent and your kids are doomed. That's your old programming. That's the voice that learned that mistakes meant you were bad. You can choose a different response. You can say, "I messed up. I'll do better next time. I'm learning."

That self-compassion is what allows you to keep going. Without it, you'll burn out. You'll give up. You'll go back to the familiar patterns because at least they feel predictable.

Stay with me here. The goal is not to never hurt your kids. The goal is to hurt them less, and to repair when you do. That's realistic. That's achievable. That's how you build a different relationship than the one you had.

What your kids actually need from you

They don't need you to be perfect. They don't need you to have all the answers. They don't need you to be their therapist or their best friend.

They need you to be present. They need you to see them. They need you to hear them. They need you to be a safe place where they can feel their feelings without being shamed for having them.

This sounds simple. It's not. Because when your child is angry, or sad, or frustrated, it might trigger something in you. You might want to fix it. You might want to make it stop. You might want to distract them or punish them or tell them they shouldn't feel that way.

That's your nervous system talking. That's your own unmet needs rising up. That's the part of you that was never allowed to have big feelings and doesn't know what to do with them now.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. He developed Internal Family Systems, which is a way of understanding that we all have different parts inside us. There's the part that gets angry when your kid is loud. There's the part that feels shame when you lose your temper. There's the part that wants to be a good parent so badly it freezes up. None of these parts are bad. They're all trying to protect you. They just need to be understood and led with compassion.

When you can be curious about your own parts, you can be curious about your child's parts too. You can see their behavior not as defiance or manipulation, but as communication from a part of them that's trying to get a need met. And that shift in perspective changes everything.

So here's what I'll leave you with. You're not going to do this perfectly. You're going to have days where you feel like you're failing. You're going to have moments where you see your parents in yourself and it breaks your heart. But you're also going to have moments where you do something different. Where you pause instead of react. Where you apologize instead of blame. Where you sit with your child's feelings instead of shutting them down.

Those moments matter. They accumulate. They build a different story for your child than the one you inherited.

And maybe, just maybe, they build a different story for you too.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Anchored by Deb Dana (paid link)
  • Radical Acceptance by Tara Brach (paid link)
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents by Lindsay Gibson (paid link)
  • No Bad Parts by Richard Schwartz (paid link)
